pull.title: kernel: Separate UTXO set access from validation functions
pull.body:
The current code makes it hard to call functions for checking the consensus-correctness of a block without having a full UTXO set at hand. This makes implementing features such as Utreexo and non-assumevalid swiftsync, where maintaining a full UTXO set defeats their purpose, through the kernel API difficult. Checks like the coinbase subsidy, or block sigops are only available through `ConnectBlock`, which requires a complete coins cache.

Solve this by moving accessing coins and the responsibility of spending them out of `ConnectBlock` and into a new separate function called `SpendBlock`. Pass a block's spent coins through the existing `CBlockUndo` data structure to `ConnectBlock`. 

While the change is largely a refactor, some smaller behaviour changes were unavoidable. These should be highlighted in the commit messages. The last commit contains the most important part of the coins logic move, the commits before attempt to prepare it piecemeal.

This pull request was benchmarked on [benchcoin](https://github.com/bitcoin-dev-tools/benchcoin/pull/162), which showed no performance regression. While discussing this pull request it was noted that pre-fetching and eliminating the need for some additional map lookups might improve performance a bit, but this does not make a measurable difference in the grand scheme of things and was not the motivation for this pull request.

In future changes, ConnectBlock could be made even more self contained, accessing the coins from the internal map could be further optimized by consolidating existence checking and spending, and the function eventually exposed in an external kernel API (as done [here](https://github.com/sedited/bitcoin/commit/09e05493010119fc60d310d3e7ffc388de441f9a#diff-f638e65d9278276b2a6ac42ffbbd26d079c3dba752feed5038a25a1ee565d320R947))

> Pass a block's spent coins through the existing `CBlockUndo` data structure to `ConnectBlock`

I don't think this is really a good direction. We already have a generic interface for supplying info about unspent coins: `CCoinsView`; I think we should be using that, rather than requiring users to convert their utxo data store into our undo format?

The thing that gets in the way of that seems to be the `AccessCoin` function returning a `Coin` by reference rather than value, but that doesn't seem particularly more efficient (it's an 8 byte copy rather than a 56 byte one unless the coin being spent is very non-standard, at least since #32279), and also seems a bit risky (in that the return value's lifetime is dependent on when the cache gets invalidated which is somewhat unpredictable).

But that seems to be fixable: https://github.com/ajtowns/bitcoin/commits/202509-ccoinsview/

(When AccessCoin was introduced in #1677, CScript always involved an additional heap allocation as it was just a `vector<char>`, so the reference would have saved an allocation. That changed a few years later when prevector was introduced in #6914 which allowed small scripts to not require an allocation. However, I haven't actually checked to see if the 8-vs-56-byte issue is actually as trivial as I think it should be)

My hesitation was mostly about re-using the coins cache as is. In my opinion it is the wrong data structure to use in this case. Potential clients of the kernel are often capable of supplying the coins in order as consumed by a transaction's inputs. Requiring these to be stored in a map seems wrong. I.e. if a utreexo client has all the leaf data to go with a transaction, it should not have to use a less efficient data structure that consumes more memory to pass in the coins. But maybe we have to build out some of the usages here in order to see what the actual drawbacks are? My current thinking is though that just passing in the required coins contiguously, i.e. use the undo data structure, is the more pragmatic solution for most external users that do not wish to implement a utxo set. 

However this is also not exactly what I think you are suggesting here, since the `CCoinsView` could eventually provide a view over contiguous data too. As implemented at the moment, that seems more confusing to use though compared to a nested vector of coins.

Another alternative solution that was suggested by cfields is introducing a new transaction type that holds the coins alongside the prevouts. I think that would be an interesting alternative too.

My goal with this PR was making the bulk of `ConnectBlock` logic work without using a `CCoinsViewCache` by moving the call to `UpdateCoins` out of the function. The side effect of that is that the `CBlockUndo` is populated outside of `ConnectBlock` too and has to be passed in. Since it already holds all the required coins, I rather took those coins vectors, than additionally requiring a view providing the same data (next to the reasons I provided in my comment above).

The change was motivated by wanting to provide a single method for validating a block with external coins, such that others can use it verbatim in the near future. The alternative of just changing some of the functions that `ConnectBlock` calls to be able to accept external coins (through either a view or contiguous data), and then splitting the rest of the `ConnectBlock` logic, such as amount, locktime, assumevalid, and validity semantics into separate functions seemed inferior to me. If an external user would want to use these functions to achieve the same outcome, a single function not only seems friendlier, but also less likely to result in bugs.

I could change this patch set to pass in a `CCoinsView` and then either handle the undo data outside of `ConnectBlock`, or populate it through the view within `ConnectBlock`.

Re https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#issuecomment-3977398080

> This seems like an extremely high risk change for very little benefit, at least for this project where we do have the UTXO set available. See previous comments at https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#issuecomment-3277131901 and I guess https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r2819423976 for an example of the riskiness.

Ok, I have to admit that I thought other projects would have picked this up by now, or at least experiment with the approach a bit, but that has not materialized so far. The immediate benefits to this project as is are moderate and motivating a significant change to consensus with a small improvement on IBD speed has landed the project in trouble before. 

However I'm still convinced that this is the architecturally sounder approach and makes things easier to reason about. That said, I would like to try out the alternative of exposing some form of coins cache wrapping instead to facilitate a fair comparison, but will wait a few days more to see what other reviewers think. Recent changes in other PRs working with the coins view cache and implementing similar facades through it (i.e. the recently introduced `CCoinsViewOverlay`) show that this might be a more palatable change.

As for the way we previously called `CheckBlock` in `ConnectBlock`, it does not bode well that a bunch of reviewers did not catch this over a few months of review. The whole logic is confusing and horrible to follow. The boolean `fChecked` traps make it hard to ascertain what is actually being checked and for which purpose. Making a failure to compute the Merkle root a shutdown, but treating others as consensus failures seems a bit inconsistent too. After all, failing to compute the Merkle root might not be related to a disk failure, but a similar "bad block" that the rest of `CheckBlock`'s logic should be guarding against.

> However I'm still convinced that this is the architecturally sounder approach and makes things easier to reason about.

I'd be interested in reviewing an approach at doing this (replacing `CoinsView` with a `span<Coins>`) just for tx (package?) validation but not for block validation, fwiw. I think that might help with disentangling the complexities of the mempool from the kernel while minimising changes/risk related to block validation.

Concept ACK.

I agree that this is scary and requires a very comprehensive review (which I have not yet done here), but I think it's worth the effort. Being able to bring your own coins to the core validation functionality would make things much easier to reason about, and is (imo) how it should've been implemented all along.

from https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#issuecomment-3277131901

> first, is that the primary client of the bitcoin core kernel is the bitcoin core node software

Is this actually true though given the bitcoinkernel project?  If other software can now import the bitcoinkernel, then there can be a multitude of other node projects interacting with the UTXO set.  How might other node projects then interact with the UTXO set?  One possibility is to just parse the blk files once they are written to disk, although this seems sub-optimal if it's only used as an intermediate step.

> Is this actually true though given the bitcoinkernel project?

If it's not true and the bitcoinkernel project is going to make it harder to maintain the node software, that seems like a big problem. :(

Thanks for taking a look @theuni,

* Took @theuni's [suggestion](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r2932834948), AccessCoins now takes a callback that it passes its retrieved coins to, which prevents any unwanted lifetime extensions.

After the recent conversation here between @sedited, @theuni, and @ajtowns, I've done further work to evaluate the motivation of this PR and wanted to share the work and my conclusions. See the work [doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1PgTLLB7HDma2nvfSCdVSJCbYE7G2scUOj5tVwWgeMGM)

From the discussion above, I think there are two motivations. 
- Goal (i) is enabling clients to supply their own coins for block validation, i.e. the immediate need for kernel API users. 

- Goal (ii) is decoupling the validation functions, improving the coins view cache, and rearchitecting the block validation flow, i.e. the broader architectural improvement. 

These goals overlap but are not in conflict, and critically, goal (i) does not require goal (ii) as a prerequisite. A kernel API could simply accept a chain tip, a block, and the coins that block consumed, and validate against those inputs directly; the internal implementation is an orthogonal concern.

I implemented two variants of block validation with caller-supplied coins.

- `TestBlockValidityWithSpentCoins`, using the coins view cache approach suggested by @ajtowns previously. I did not use the proposed BlockUndo wrapper to CCoinsView as I find it similar in effect to simply having the caller pass a list of coins that are appended to the view, which is what this approach does: https://github.com/ismaelsadeeq/bitcoin/tree/04-2026-test-block-validity-spentouts-rebased. 

- `TestBlockValidityWithSpentCoinsUndo`, building on this PR's approach: https://github.com/ismaelsadeeq/bitcoin/tree/pr-32317.

Both variants have been exposed through the Bitcoin kernel C header API, making it technically feasible for client projects such as SwiftSync or Utreexo to call either path. Note that headers must be synced first; the precondition is documented in the API. I also opened #35000 with unit tests for block validation that are useful independently of the outcome of this PR.

I now think the two goals motivating this PR should be pursued separately. Goal (i) does not require the architectural refactor in this PR as a prerequisite; the coins view approach achieves this trivially, as is evident in the POC above. Goal (ii) is valuable and worth pursuing, but should be done incrementally with rigorous fuzz and unit tests at each step, as raised by @ajtowns and @theuni and in light of the recent bugs found by @w0xlt.

I am inclined to suggest that the refactoring work in goal (ii) proceed methodically once we have expanded the tests in #35000 to cover not just `TestBlockValidity` but test/simulate other validation paths, so that we can catch bugs early. Something like #34895 should similarly be expanded to cover block validity and other block validation paths beyond just `ConnectBlock` see https://github.com/ismaelsadeeq/bitcoin/tree/fixup-connect-block-harness  that expanded it to test block validity and testblockvalidity with spent outputs.

As a concrete demonstration that testing can de-risk this work: the bug caught by @w0xlt at https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r2819423976 was reproduced by the unit and fuzz tests after I substituted the vulnerable commit into https://github.com/ismaelsadeeq/bitcoin/tree/pr-32317. I expect the same approach would catch https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r3070009097 and any other hidden issues (if they exist), as long as we simulate all the block validation paths with their specific quirks and feed them sufficiently adversarial blocks.

Thank you for the review and test case @w0xlt!

In 2be3c59151bcb840eb8cbd045a3dada6775ebc9e "validation: Add SpendBlock function": this or the final commit would be a good time to document both `SpendBlock` and `ConnectBlock`.

IIUC `SpendBlock`:

1. Checks that all spent coins existed
 i) including coins created and spent within the block, in that order
3. Optimistically updates the UTXO set with newly created coins
 i) checks BIP30 (not in this order, but conceptually)
4. Populates Undo data

And then `ConnectBlock` does all the other consensus checks (like verifying the input scripts). It uses the Undo data populated by `SpendBlock` and does _not_ need the UTXO set.

comments.body: My read is that the call to `txdata.Init` is delayed to after checking the script execution cache for performance reasons. Calling `Init` does some hashing, that would not be required if it is already in the cache. Moving the `spent_outputs` vector construction is already a potential performance downside for potential cache hits, though I think that is more acceptable in the grand scheme of things.

nit: grammar change to highlight that `pindex` can be mutated here
```suggestion
     * @param[out] pindex     Points to the block map entry associated with block. On successful validation, its nStatus block validity is raised
```

In a way, it's a bit silly to add a parameter to the `CheckInputScripts` signature to then just move it into another parameter (`txdata`). An alternative approach could be to let the caller handle this, and `assert txdata.m_spent_outputs_ready`, but that could raise runtime assertion errors instead of catching them at compile time.

As @TheCharlatan noted [here](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r2083133917), delaying `txdata.Init` could be on purpose for the potential cache hit [here](https://github.com/TheCharlatan/bitcoin/blob/16a695fbff4a92eba3eb72ec6aa766e71c52f773/src/validation.cpp#L2195:L2201). I wonder if there is a cleaner version for achieving the same goal though.
It compiles for me as well.

Looks like we have an early return in `GetTransactionSigOpCost` for coinbase tx and no use for `coins` span; so maybe we could just pass an empty span here?
```suggestion
            nSigOpsCost += GetTransactionSigOpCost<const Coin>(tx, {}, flags);
```

Do we need to update the [documentation](https://github.com/TheCharlatan/bitcoin/blob/16a695fbff4a92eba3eb72ec6aa766e71c52f773/src/consensus/tx_verify.h#L24:L31) for `CheckTxInputs` now that the input existence check is removed from it?

In commit 5908680, the description mentions: "By returning early in case of a BIP30 validation failure, an additional failure check for an invalid block reward in case of its failure is left out."

To validate my understanding: there are also other checks in `ConnectBlock` before the `!state.IsValid()` check that we may have skipped because of the early return when `SpendBlock` fails - including transaction input validation, script verification, fee range checks, ...; Is that correct?

It makes sense to me not to proceed with additional validation if `SpendBlock` already detects a consensus failure, but I'm curious why the previous code continued checking for other consensus violations even after detecting an early BIP30 failure?

Pushed the compiler issue on a separate branch here: https://github.com/TheCharlatan/bitcoin/actions/runs/15016860100/job/42196724477#step:10:198 .

> edit: I see this is already (partially) suggested and addressed in https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r2078426205, I think adding the IsCoinRef concept could still be nice but less important than specifying the std::span

I did implement a concept initially, but did not find it really that helpful, since the two usable template variants are concretely defined anyway.

Imo, there are 2 main advantages:
- discoverability: grepping for callsites, template instantiations etc is a lot more cumbersome (and easy to miss) than just inspecting the canonical template definition
- type safety: explicitly narrowing down the actual types that `T` can be makes reviewing the function easier, because you have to think about edge cases less, e.g. will the function still be safe when a new callsite is added

This is more a general style preference, and not particularly important for this PR, so feel free to ignore / can mark as resolved.

comments.body:
> There are also other checks in ConnectBlock before the !state.IsValid() check that we may have skipped because of the early return when SpendBlock fails - including transaction input validation, script verification, fee range checks, ...; Is that correct?

Good catch! Actually I think the commit message description is misleading, if not wrong. The checks you mention are skipped in any case, because we check `if (!state.IsValid()) break;`, but similarly the coinbase check won't override the validation result if it is already invalid. The check is still executed though before returning.

It seems like list initializing `coin` is triggering MSVC to copy-initialize a `Coin` temporary, causing a second user-defined conversion and subsequent error. I don't really understand why direct initialization rules are different in this regard, but using it does seem to make MSVC happy: https://github.com/stickies-v/bitcoin/actions/runs/15018875180/job/42203094843

Fix: https://github.com/stickies-v/bitcoin/commit/6f1bc11c336db6dcea2954a03520b7064f0f0e6d
Sweet, will apply this!

We could also use `AccessCoin`, but I think accessing it directly is a bit more efficient, because we don't allocate another vector. Do you have a better suggestion for the naming there? It is all a bit confusing.
Thanks, added the the concept.

Since we are doing this,  I think this should also be separated into two functions.
 1. `EnforceBIP30` — a method that performs BIP30 validation.
 2. `SpendBlock` — for each transaction in the block, checks that the outputs it is spending exist, spends them, and populates the `CBlockUndo` data structure.

It would also make testing easier, and removing BIP30 after a consensus cleanup soft fork would be straightforward.

What I will want to see is we have just SpendBlock and  non-contextual block validation using the block data and undo data in a series of consensus rules validation functions.

In "validation: Add SpendBlock function" c937b12745971435111eab948e505580754d56bd
why duplicate this, I think we should just add a comment warning that we expect they are already executed before?

In "validation: Add SpendBlock function" c937b12745971435111eab948e505580754d56bd

> The goal is to move logic requiring access to the
CCoinsViewCache out of ConnectBlock and to the new SpendBlock method.
SpendBlock will in future handle all UTXO set interactions that
previously took place in ConnectBlock.

This is counter to the objective of the PR; we are still accessing the UTXO set in this block validation function.
My hope was that we would have two UTXO-accessing validation functions that 1 populate the block undo data and 2. BIP30 separately, while the remaining validation functions would simply get passed the block undo data and operate as stateless validation functions.
I'm not sure what you mean here. Afaict this is moved over from `ConnectBlock`.

It is important that the BIP30 validation is done before we spend the coins. The current split makes sense to me for this reason. Can you elaborate a bit more what you think might not be optimal here?

I'm still not sure if this is a good thing. I don't see why you would ever spend the coins in the utxo set without also doing the bip30 checks beforehand. To this end, I did implement it, but added a tag type that gets passed from BIP30Validate to SpendBlock: https://github.com/sedited/bitcoin/tree/spendblock_17 . Let me know what you think.

I think I'd rather keep these duplicated for now. These are short-circuits to our logic, similar to how the coinbase checks work in various places. Based on that I'd rather keep them in place. This could also easily be improved in a follow-up.

I’ve reviewed the branch, and it looks good overall. My only concern is the last commit that adds the tag. Initially, I thought it should be the caller’s responsibility to decide whether `bip30validate` is called, rather than mandating it here.

My motivation for separating `SpendBlock` from `BIP30Validate` is discussed in:
https://github.com/stratum-mining/sv2-apps/issues/120

The branch without the last commit would make it easier to implement a variant of the idea described in https://github.com/stratum-mining/sv2-apps/issues/120, where  block validation for this JDS is as follows call `SpendBlock` and then `ConnectBlock` skip BIP30. However, the idea of skipping consensus checks is not acceptable.

Given that, I’m inclined to keep this as-is.
For https://github.com/stratum-mining/sv2-apps/issues/120, we can instead expose IPC methods: `SpendBlock`, which takes a block and returns the coins, and `ValidateBlock`, which performs the full block validation checks. Since blocks from miners tend to share many transactions, they can still benefit from this approach, because the JDS needs to only call `SpendBlock` to validate newly seen transactions.

For Utreexo client, they provide their own undo data and cannot perform BIP30 validation anyway, so they benefit from this approach as well.

Overall, this is still an improvement over the current status quo.

Thanks for catching this! ~I wonder if the better approach would be to move the defensive `CheckBlock` call to `SpendBlock` and assert that `fChecked` is set in `ConnectBlock`. What do you think?~
EDIT: I'll stick to your suggestion, mine introduces some unexpected complexity with the unchecked states.

Why was it changed from `assert` to `Assume` ?
With this, block/pindex mismatch will silently proceed through consensus-critical validation.
I reverted it.
Same as https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/32317#discussion_r2874378016
Fixed.
It seems the empty block validation from https://github.com/sedited/bitcoin/compare/spendblock_19..spendblock_20 was removed.

Yes, I kept the test, but chose to move `CheckBlock` to the new `SpendBlock` after all. The reason why I think this one-off length check is probably not what we want to do, is that `CheckBlock` would normally first compute the merkle root of the block. If it fails to do that, for example through a missing transaction, we currently shutdown and don't mark the block as permanently invalid. This additional length check might shadow that condition.

@ajtowns that's neat :)

I'd be curious to see what IBD performance looked like doing (speculative) block validation in batches with no io per-batch.

But, for the sake of this PR, I think we should separate the concerns: data retrieval/availability, and what we could actually _do_ with better availability.

(incoming thought dump after poking around here today. Not fully fleshed out)

tl;dr: I think it's worth experimenting with having the db pass shared pointers to coins and letting `CCoinsViewCache` store them that way. That way we can pass them out with no lifetime concerns, and use the data elsewhere.

This PR purports to "separate UTXO set access from validation functions", though in some cases (mempool) it just hides the accesses via references to cached entries.

I think there's another way that _fully_ severs the db (cache) dependency for data availability: have `CCoinsViewCache` store a `std::shared_ptr<const Coin>` rather than just a bare `Coin`.  Then `AccessCoins` can return a `std::vector<std::shared_ptr<const Coin>>`. That way there are no lifetime concerns.

I believe that would make life easier for @sedited here, and for you too @ajtowns, as it untangles availability vs what's active.

Imo we want new types: `ContextualTransaction` and `ContextualBlock`. `SpendBlock()` would take a `CBlock` as input, do the lookups, transform each `CTransaction` into a corresponding `ContextualTransaction`, with `shared_ptr<Coin>`s added to the inputs. Then a `ContextualBlock` is created from those. SpendBlock now creates a `ContextualBlock` rather than the awkward `CBlock` and `CBlockUndo` and passes it to `ConnectBlock`.

I realize I'm getting ahead of myself there, but it seems inevitable that we'll eventually want to be able to pass blocks with pre-filled input data to/from the kernel. With that assumption in mind, I'd rather just go ahead and commit to that than abuse `CBlockUndo`.

Thank you! That test seems like something we should add independently of this PR. Do you want to open a fresh PR for it? I can also do it, if you don't have the immediate capacity.

EDIT: It is also interesting that this is apparently the first test we have triggering this class of error. Or at least, it is currently the only test that prints an error string, which makes me suspect a suppression for it was not required by the other tests.
Thanks! I can open a new PR for that test if needed, but since it’s already included here, I think it fits well with these changes.
